title: P.T.O (Preference, Tolerance, Objections) Team Game
body:
Team Toolbox

P.T.O Team Game Preference, Tolerance, Objections

This is a game to learn about group decision making. Is design for groups of 4-7 people, and will take 35 minutes minimum, but you can play as long as you want. It’s a great way to experience the different collaborative decision-making methods you’ve been learning about. In a larger group you can run it in parallel on different tables and compare notes after.

You will need: ● 5 different coloured tokens (e.g. small post-it notes or game counters) for each player, each colour representing a position people can take on the given scenario. ● A large sheet of paper with a drawing of the overlapping circles diagram:

Online variation: if you are meeting online, you can use a whiteboard tool like Miro or Mural instead of paper & tokens.

Phases of the game:

1. Establish the scenario - 5 minutes 2. Taking positions - 3 minutes 3. Deciding - 20 minutes 4. Debrief - 5 minutes  Phase 1. Establish the Scenario --

Scenario: The messy kitchen Your team lives together. The kitchen is always messy. You will decide together how to solve the challenge. Take 5min to build your shared reality by considering the following questions:

● What's a scenario where your characters would be living together? e.g. is it a student ﬂat, retirement village, a remote community in a distant planet … ● What is unique about the kitchen you have? ● Who keeps the oddest hours in the house? ● …or, can you think of any other questions that will help you build a sense of who you are as a group?

Before moving to the next step, choose which decision method you will play with (Consensus, Consent, Advice)

Phase 2. Taking positions ------- --

Read together all the proposals below and consider your position for each (object, tolerate, or prefer). But don’t place them on the board yet!

● The people who care about the mess should be the ones to clean. [Orange token] ● A cleaning roster: one member cleans the kitchen each week. [Blue token] ● Monitor the kitchen with security cameras to track who is making the mess, so we can organise punishments and rewards. [Green token] ● We should all contribute extra money to pay someone to clean. [Yellow token] ● The men should clean the kitchen to compensate for patriarchy. [Pink token]

Once everyone has made their choices, count to 3 and every player will simultaneously place their 5 colour tokens (representing each option) on the board. Eg, if you prefer option 1 and 2 place them on the ‘I Prefer’ section. If everyone prefers the same option, you can move the tokens from ‘I Prefer’ to ‘We Prefer’.  Phase 3. Make a decision -------- --

You have 20 minutes to make a decision, follow the rules for the method you chose to play with.

Consensus game - We look for a decision everyone will prefer.

1. Start with a round where everyone shares the reasoning behind their positions. 2. Then each player may choose to move any of their tokens, to reﬂect how their position has changed after hearing others. (If any preference or tolerance is shared by all players, move those tokens to the ‘we prefer’ or ‘we tolerate’ sections) 3. Anyone can raise a proposal, based on what you see on the board. 4. Everyone votes with their hands (thumbs up = agree; thumb sideways = I don’t prefer it but I can live with it; thumbs down = I object) 5. Hear from everyone who does not fully support this proposal, about how it can be improved. 6. Proposal is amended until everyone is satisﬁed this is the best decision we could come to.

Consent game - We look for a decision that nobody objects to.

1. Based on the board, everyone has 1 minute to think of a proposal to present to the group. Once you have a proposal in mind, raise your hand. The person that raised their hand ﬁrst is the one that will share their proposal. 2. Once the presenter has ﬁnished sharing their proposal, have a round to ask clarifying questions and share initial reactions. 3. Then each player votes with either a thumbs up for ‘This proposal is good for now, safe to try’, or a thumbs down for ‘I object’. 4. Anyone that objects, has 1’ to share why they object and if they have a suggestion for improving the proposal. 5. Anyone can raise a new proposal for voting. You continue with voting and amended proposals until you ﬁnd a solution that nobody objects to.  Advice game - We empower someone to make a well informed decision. 1. Take a moment to look at the board, based on what you know about the group and yourself: consider how conﬁdent you feel about being the decision maker for everyone. Rank your conﬁdence by placing 1 to 5 ﬁngers in front of you (keep it there). 2. Once everyone has ranked their conﬁdence, count to 3 together and use your other hand to point toward the person you think should be the decision maker (it will be the person that has the most ﬁngers pointed at them ;). 3. Based on the positions on the board, the decision maker has 3’ to ask clarifying questions about some of the positions. 4. Now, the decision maker has 1-2’ to think about 2 questions they will ask the whole group, to gain the information or advice they need. 5. Take turns to share answers, one round per question. Decision maker may adjust their question after the ﬁrst round. 6. Decision maker has a few minutes to consider the points they heard and make the decision. Sharing back why they decided the way they did.

Phase 4. Debrief-------- --

Take 5 minutes to debrief the game. Depending on the method you choose to play with follow the prompts below.

If you chose Consensus: Were you able to reach a consensus on the map? Would more or less rounds help this process? How well do you feel you did at saying no / putting thumbs down when you wanted to? And saying ‘yes’ / thumbs up?

How would you approach Consensus decision making in your real groups now? What would you do differently?  If you chose Consent: How many different proposals or amendments did it take to ﬁnd a ‘good for now safe to try’ decision? How easy was it to let go of trying to ﬁnd a ‘better decision’ instead of a ‘good enough’ one? How well do you feel you did at saying no / putting thumbs down when you wanted to? And saying ‘yes’ / thumbs up?

How would you approach Consent decision making in your real groups now? What would you do differently?

If you chose Advice: What did you base your conﬁdence on making a decision? How did your fellow players’ projected conﬁdence (with their ﬁngers) impact who you chose to be the decision maker? Did you feel listened to? Do you feel you were a good listener? Did you feel the method gave you a fair outcome?

How would you approach Advice decision making in your real groups now? What would you do differently?
